title: Printer's Triple-Bay Cabinet
summary: A wide three-bay cabinet with two door compartments and a center of stacked drawers, faced with printer's-drawer-style 1x3/1x2 grids — build every drawer and door to your own actual openings.
description:
This cabinet is built from two matching 2x2 face frames — front and back — connected by 2x2 corner posts, with plywood sides and dividers ripped into 15-3/4"-wide strips. **Build both face frames identically except the back one, which skips the middle drawer dividers** since it doesn't need to frame individual drawer faces the way the front does.

Build the doors before final assembly, using the front frame flat on the bench to line up all eleven door-face boards square — trying to square eleven thin boards after the cabinet is standing is much harder than doing it flat. **Connect the two frames with glue and screws, checking for square at every stage** — sides, then dividers, keeping the dividers flush to the inside of each compartment.

**Every drawer must be built to suit your actual finished opening and your actual slides, not the plan's stated numbers** — the given measurements assume perfect boards and slides needing exactly 1/2" clearance, which real lumber rarely delivers. Slide support cleats only go in the two outer compartments; the center compartment doesn't need them. Install each drawer inset 3/4" for the drawer face, then attach the faces with an even 1/8" gap on all sides. The back panel and shelves are both optional — add a 1/4" plywood back cut to the overall size minus 1/2" if you want one, and shelves either fixed or on shelf pins.

tools.note: For ripping the plywood into strips.

materials.name: Euro style bottom corner mount drawer slides, 16 in, pair
3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Build to suit your actual opening and slides.
Build to suit your actual opening and slides.

steps.title: Rip the plywood
steps.body: Rip the plywood sheet into 15-3/4"-wide strips for the sides, dividers, shelves, and corner posts.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Cut the frame and face stock
steps.body: From the 2x2 cut the face frame stiles 72" long, eight rails 34 1/2", and nine dividers 22" — all nine dividers go in the front frame, since the back frame is built without them. From the 1x3 cut twenty-four wide face boards 21 3/4"; from the 1x2 cut eighteen narrow face boards 21 3/4", six cross pieces 22 1/2", and four drawer slide supports 15 3/4". Crosscut the ripped plywood strips into two sides 69", four dividers and shelves 31", and four corner posts 15 3/4" square. Two counts are worth checking before you buy: the cut list gives only two 72" stiles, but the front and back frames need a pair each, so cut four — and four stiles pushes the 2x2 requirement to nine 8 ft boards against the eight listed. The 1x2 is short as listed too; the narrow face boards, cross pieces and slide supports need seven 8 ft boards, not six.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Build the two face frames
steps.body: Assemble each frame flat on the bench with two 72" stiles on the outside and the 34 1/2" rails between them, which makes each frame 37 1/2" wide overall. Set the pocket-hole jig for 1 1/2" stock, drill the ends of every rail and divider, and join with 2 1/2" pocket hole screws and glue. Space the four rails to give the three bay heights you want, then fit the nine 22" dividers into the front frame only — the back frame gets rails and stiles alone, because it never has to frame an individual drawer face. Measure both diagonals on each finished frame and rack it until they match; a frame that is out of square here cannot be corrected once the cabinet is standing.

steps.title: Build the doors and test drawer faces
steps.body: Lay the front frame flat and build the door faces directly against it, using the 21 3/4" wide and narrow boards with the 22 1/2" cross pieces behind them — the frame is your reference surface, and squaring eight thin boards against it now is far easier than trying to true them up after the cabinet is standing. Glue and pin each board to its cross pieces, keeping the gaps between boards consistent across the whole face. Dry-lay the drawer face boards in their openings at the same time and mark which group belongs to which drawer, because the openings will not all come out identical.

steps.title: Connect the frames
steps.body: Stand the two frames parallel, 15 3/4" apart — the width of the ripped plywood — and join them at each corner with a 15 3/4" plywood corner post, glued and screwed into both frames. Work on a flat floor rather than a sagging bench, because a twist locked in now shows up later as doors that will not sit flush. Check the diagonals across the top and again across the bottom before the glue grabs; equal diagonals are the only reliable proof the box is square.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Add the sides
steps.body: Set each 69" plywood side into the frames so it runs between the top and bottom rails — 69" plus the two 1 1/2" rails is the frame's full 72" height, which is how you know the side is the right part and the right way up. Keep its outside face flush with the outside of the stiles, then glue and screw through the side into the stiles and corner posts. Check the cabinet for square and each side for plumb as you fasten, not after.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Add the dividers
steps.body: Stand the 31" plywood dividers upright between the sides to split the cabinet into its three bays, keeping each one flush to the inside face of the front frame so nothing proud of it can foul a drawer or a door. Fasten with pocket holes from the back side, or with screws down through the rails, holding a square against the divider and the frame as you drive the first fastener. Then measure the finished opening in every bay and write the numbers down — every drawer and door from here on is built to those measurements, not to the plan's.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Add the drawer slide supports
steps.body: Fasten the four 15 3/4" 1x2 slide supports inside the two outer bays only — the centre bay's dividers already give the slides something to screw to. Run each support front to back, its full 15 3/4" matching the cabinet's depth so it lands flush at both ends, and set the pair in each bay level with one another. Check with a level across the two supports before the glue sets; a slide mounted on a support that slopes will bind under load no matter how well the drawer is built.

steps.title: Build the drawers
steps.body: Build three drawer boxes from the 1x6, sizing each to the opening you measured minus whatever side clearance your slides require. Do not simply cut to the listed 19 1/2" sides and 17" fronts and backs — those assume perfectly flat boards and slides needing exactly 1/2" a side, which real lumber and real hardware rarely deliver. Join the corners with glue and 1 1/4" pocket hole screws, or glue and brads if you would rather not see pocket holes inside the box. Measure the diagonals on each box before the glue sets; a racked box binds in its slides however carefully the slides are mounted.

steps.title: Install the drawers and hardware
steps.body: Mount the Euro bottom-corner slides to the 1x2 supports in the two outer bays and to the divider faces in the centre bay, keeping each pair level and at the same height front to back. Slide each box in so its front sits 3/4" back from the face frame — exactly the thickness of the face boards that go on next. Hang the doors on the inset hinges last, shimming each until the gap around it looks even on all four sides, and check every drawer runs to full extension before you commit to the hinge screw positions.
materials: Euro style bottom corner mount drawer slides, 16 in, pair

steps.title: Attach the drawer faces
steps.body: Fasten each assembled face panel to its drawer box from inside the box, so no fastener shows on the front. Set the face with an even 1/8" gap on all four sides, using scraps or coins of that thickness as shims under and beside it while you drive the first two screws, rather than eyeballing it. Open and close each drawer before moving to the next; across a run of faces this size, one set a hair out of line is obvious from across the room.

steps.title: Add the top and shelves
steps.body: Add the top. Add shelves, fixed or on shelf pins, and the optional back panel with nails and glue.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand all surfaces, apply primer and paint or stain and poly, and add the cup handles.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Printer's triple-bay cabinet with doors and drawers
